Document [1] (Title: Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy -- Indications) Chimeric antigen receptors usually contain an extracellular domain that binds to a specific antigen on tumor cells, a transmembrane domain, and intracellular domains that signal T-cell activation to attack tumor cells. Tisagenlecleucel is a cluster of differentiation (CD) 19-directed genetically modified autologous T-cell immunotherapy that involves reprogramming a patient’s T-cells with a transgene encoding a CAR to identify and eliminate CD19-expressing cells, both malignant and normal. The CAR comprises a murine single-chain antibody fragment; this recognizes CD19 and fuses to intracellular signaling domains from 4-1BB (CD137) and CD3 zeta. The CD3 zeta component is critical for initiating T-cell activation and anti-tumor activity, while 4-1BB enhances the expansion and persistence of tisagenlecleucel. Upon binding to CD19-expressing cells, the CAR transmits a signal to promote T-cell expansion, activation, target cell elimination, and persistence of the tisagenlecleucel cells. Axicabtagene ciloleucel is another CD19-directed genetically modified autologous T-cell immunotherapy that binds to CD19-expressing cancer cells and normal B cells. Following anti-CD19 CAR T-cell engagement with CD19-expressing target cells, the CD28 and CD3-zeta co-stimulatory domains activate downstream signaling cascades that lead to T-cell activation, proliferation, acquisition of effector functions and secretion of inflammatory cytokines and chemokines. This sequence of events leads to the killing of CD19-expressing cells. [3] [4] [5] [6] [7]

Document [3] (Title: CART Cell Therapy Toxicity -- Introduction) Clusters of differentiation-19 (CD-19), a B cell surface marker, -directed CAR-T cells show encouraging outcomes in a variety of malignancies, including pediatric and B-cell acute lymphocytic leukemia (ALL), non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL), and chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL). The first approved CAR-T therapy targeting CD-19, tisagenlecleucel (tisa-cel, ELIANA), was initially approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for patients with ALL in 2017, and later diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), high-grade B-cell lymphoma, and DLBCL arising from follicular lymphoma (JULIET). [6] [7] [8] Around the same time, axicabtagene ciloleucel (axi-cel, ZUMA-1) gained approval for relapsed/refractory (R/R) DLBCL and later for R/R follicular lymphoma. [9] [10]

Document [5] (Title: Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy -- Clinical Significance) The first CAR-T cell treatment approved by the United States Food and Drug Administration(FDA) in August 2017 was tisagenlecleucel. It is indicated in patients up to 25 years of age with B-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukemia that is refractory or in the second or later relapse. It also has indications in adult patients with relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma after 2 or more lines of systemic therapy, including diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) not otherwise specified, high-grade B-cell lymphoma and DLBCL arising from follicular lymphoma. The second US FDA-approved CAR-T cell treatment, in October 2017, is axicabtagene ciloleucel, which is indicated for the treatment of adult patients with relapsed or refractory large B-cell lymphoma after 2 or more lines of systemic therapy, including DLBCL not otherwise specified, primary mediastinal large B-cell lymphoma, high-grade B-cell lymphoma, and DLBCL arising from follicular lymphoma. Neither treatment indicates patients with primary central nervous system(CNS) lymphoma. According to the US National Institute of Health clinical trial registration, multiple clinical trials are underway with CAR-T cell treatment for different malignancies, including multiple myeloma, CNS tumors, hepatocellular carcinoma, lung cancer, etc. [3] [4] [5] [6]

Document [8] (Title: Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management -- B Cell Lymphoma) Diffuse Large B cell lymphoma: In Stage I or II, the R-CHOP regimen is often given for 3 to 6 cycles, with/without radiation therapy to the lymph node that is affected. In stage III or IV, 6 cycles of R-CHOP is the preferred treatment. Imaging tests such as PET/CT scans are done to evaluate treatment response after 2-4 cycles. Intrathecal chemotherapy or high doses of methotrexate intravenously is given to patients in the presence or at high risk of central nervous system involvement. For "Double-hit" lymphomas, that is, with translocations of MYC and BCL2 or BCL6 as detected by FISH or standard cytogenetics, DA-EPOCH-R is preferred. Refractory or relapsed diffuse large B cell lymphoma can be treated with a salvage regimen followed by bone marrow transplant for eligible patients or Chimeric antigen receptor T cell (CAR-T) cell therapy. [17] CAR-T therapy is a form of immunotherapy in which the patient's T lymphocytes are genetically modified with a gene that encodes a CAR that targets the patient's cancer. CAR-T therapy has shown effectiveness against refractory CD19-expressing B lymphoid malignancies. [18]
Document [9] (Title: Immunotherapy -- Indications -- CAR T-cell therapy) B-cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (B-ALL): CAR T-cell therapy has demonstrated remarkable efficacy in treating relapsed or refractory B-ALL, particularly in pediatric and young adult patients. Diffuse Large B-cell Lymphoma (DLBCL): CAR T-cell therapy has been approved for certain subtypes of DLBCL, including relapsed or refractory DLBCL after 2 or more lines of systemic therapy. Follicular Lymphoma (FL): CAR T-cell therapy has demonstrated promising results in treating relapsed or refractory FL, especially in patients who have failed multiple lines of therapy. Mantle Cell Lymphoma (MCL): CAR T-cell therapy has shown efficacy in treating relapsed or refractory MCL, particularly in patients who have failed prior therapies. Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL): While still investigational, CAR T-cell therapy is being studied in CLL, particularly in patients with high-risk disease or those who have relapsed after multiple lines of therapy.

Document [12] (Title: Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy -- Continuing Education Activity) Chimeric antigen receptors (CARs) are recombinant receptors for antigens that redirect the specificity and function of T lymphocytes or other immune cells in a single molecule. The concept of using CARs in cancer immunotherapy is that CARs, which are programmed to target tumor-associated antigens, can be replicated rapidly and homogeneously. Direct infusion of these armed tumor-targeting T cells bypasses the barriers and kinetics of active immunization. This activity describes the indications, contraindications, and complications of CART treatment and highlights the interprofessional team's role in managing cancer patients.
Document [13] (Title: Immunotherapy -- Indications -- CAR T-cell therapy) In addition to these hematological malignancies, CAR T-cell therapy is also being investigated for certain types of solid tumors, although progress in this area has been more limited. Solid tumors being targeted with CAR T-cell therapy include: Multiple Myeloma: CAR T-cell therapy targeting BCMA (B-cell maturation antigen) has shown promise in treating relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma. Glioblastoma: CAR T-cell therapy targeting EGFRvIII (epidermal growth factor receptor variant III) has been investigated in glioblastoma, although results have been mixed. Sarcomas: CAR T-cell therapy is being explored in certain types of sarcomas, including osteosarcoma and synovial sarcoma, although research is still in the early stages.

Document [18] (Title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Clinical Significance -- Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy) Chimeric antigen receptor T-cell (CAR-T cell) therapy is a form of adoptive immunotherapy that involves the transfer of genetically modified T cells to stimulate an anti-leukemic immune response. For patients at very high risk of relapse or those who have relapsed after initial treatment, CAR-T cell therapy presents a promising option. This approach modifies a patient's T cells to specifically target leukemia cells. CAR-T cell therapy has demonstrated significant success in treating relapsed or refractory ALL, especially in patients with CD19-positive B-ALL. [80]

Document [25] (Title: Relapsed and Refractory Follicular Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management) CAR-T therapy is an option for patients with multiply relapsed follicular lymphomas. CAR-T cells are created from the patient's T-lymphocytes that have been genetically modified (transfected) with a gene encoding a CAR to direct the T-cells against the lymphoma cells. Document [27] (Title: CART Cell Therapy Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity) Chimeric antigen receptor-T (CAR-T) cell therapy is an evolving treatment entity used to treat relapsed/refractory cancers. It involves genetically modifying autologous or allogeneic T cells to express Chimeric Antigen Receptor (CAR) proteins that target specific cancer cells. Despite promising outcomes, these cells attack normal tissue cells expressing similar proteins on their surfaces. This cross-reactivity results in adverse events (AEs), leading to substantial mortality and morbidity. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of CAR-T toxicity and highlights the role of interprofessional teams in evaluating and treating patients with these AEs.

Document [31] (Title: Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy -- Equipment) For administering both CAR-T cell treatments, it is important to confirm the patient’s identity with the patient identifiers on the infusion bag, as they are all for autologous use. A leuodepleting filter cannot be used, and central venous access is the recommended method for infusion. Interleukin-6 antagonists (ie, tocilizumab or siltuximab), corticosteroids, and emergent equipment are needed before infusion and during the recovery period in case of side effects.

Document [2] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214) Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.
Document [3] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48) BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.

Document [9] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214) The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.

Document [13] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916) Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.
Document [14] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286) Imatinib is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor, meaning it belongs to the class of drugs that specifically target BCR‑ABL fusion proteins. BCR‑ABL fusion proteins serve as the molecular target for BCR‑ABL inhibitors; they are oncogenic drivers created by the Philadelphia chromosome translocation and are central to the pathogenesis of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By binding to and inhibiting these fusion proteins, imatinib and other BCR‑ABL inhibitors can suppress the abnormal signaling that promotes uncontrolled cell proliferation, thereby providing an effective targeted therapy for patients with these malignancies.

Document [19] (Title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 37) BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib; they inhibit the BCR‑ABL tyrosine‑kinase pathway and are employed in hematologic malignancies, with dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib and imatinib mesylát indicated for lymphoid leukemia and ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, another protein‑kinase inhibitor subgroup, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib and target Bruton's tyrosine‑kinase; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia, highlighting BTK inhibition in lymphoid cancers. EGFR inhibitors such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class and exemplify kinase‑targeted therapy beyond leukemias. The ATC category “Other protein kinase inhibitors” also contains agents such as cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin have been identified as potential treatments for acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these protein‑kinase inhibitors—targeting BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases—constitute a network of antineoplastic and immunomodulating drugs that underpin contemporary therapeutic strategies for lymphoid and myeloid leukemias and other cancers.
Document [20] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 75) BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein‑kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, comprise dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي), whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). The “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” group, encompassing cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin, belongs to the same protein‑kinase‑inhibitor class and is employed to inhibit kinase signaling in cancer and to modulate immune responses. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically recognized for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Collectively, these agents illustrate how distinct kinase‑targeted drugs are organized within broader therapeutic families and linked to the management of various hematologic malignancies.

This integrated framework delineates classifications, qualifying relationships, therapeutic scopes and specific contraindications of key antineoplastic and inhibitory drugs, supporting informed oncologic patient care.
Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 764) Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. EGFR inhibitors are a subset of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, placing them within the broader therapeutic category of agents used to treat cancer and modulate immune responses.
Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 89) The compound 4‑aminopyrazolo(3,4‑d)pyrimidine is classified as an anticholesteremic agent, and within this anticholesteremic category a subcategory called hematologic agents (hematologické látky) is defined. Hematologic agents include drugs such as zanubrutinib, filgrastim, and protamines. Zanubrutinib, together with ibrutinib, belongs to the class of Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors; BTK inhibitors are themselves a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that have antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Ibrutinib is indicated for the treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Filgrastim, also a hematologic agent, is used to stimulate white‑blood‑cell production, and protamines represent another type of hematologic agent. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors constitute another group of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are examples of EGFR inhibitors. A broader grouping labeled “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” also falls under the protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents class. This group includes cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin have therapeutic activity against acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Overall, the taxonomy links several targeted therapies—BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other protein kinase inhibitors—to their respective clinical indications in hematologic malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia, while also situating them within broader pharmacologic categories like anticholesteremic agents and protein kinase inhibitor classes.

Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437) Imatinib, also known as imatinib mesylate, is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor; it belongs to the class of agents that target the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase and is used therapeutically to inhibit this oncogenic driver in relevant hematologic malignancies.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 328) Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are a class of protein kinase inhibitors that also function as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Both ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members of this BTK inhibitor class. Ibrutinib, as a BTK inhibitor, may be used in the treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن).
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 751) Crizanlizumab is identified as a selective adhesion molecule inhibitor, placing it within the product class of selective adhesion molecule inhibitors. In addition, crizanlizumab is classified as both an immunomodulatory agent (imunomodulátory) and an immunosuppressive agent (imunosupresiva), reflecting its dual capacity to modify and dampen immune system activity. These properties align crizanlizumab with the broader disposition of immunomodulators, indicating that its therapeutic action involves regulation of immune responses through targeted inhibition of adhesion molecules. This integrated classification underscores crizanlizumab’s role in modulating immune pathways and its potential utility in conditions where controlling immune cell adhesion and activity is clinically beneficial.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 326) Ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Both ponatinib and its hydrochloride salt share the same indication, reflecting that the active compound is effective in this hematologic malignancy. Asciminib, a distinct BCR‑ABL1 inhibitor, also offers a treatment option for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, particularly those with resistance to earlier tyrosine‑kinase inhibitors. The availability of multiple agents targeting the same disease provides clinicians with alternative strategies to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1288) Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this therapeutic class, zanubrutinib is a specific BTK inhibitor that exemplifies the targeted approach of these agents. By inhibiting BTK, these drugs are employed in the treatment of certain malignancies and immune‑mediated conditions, reflecting their role as both anticancer and immune‑modulating therapies.
